result.vlm_summary:
<TRAJECTA_DATA_91c1abd0>page_state:
Google Flights results page for one-way Sydney → San Francisco on Tue, Mar 10. “Best” tab selected; top flights list visible with prices and durations.
task_relevant_visible_text:
“One way”, “Sydney”, “San Francisco”, “Tue, Mar 10”, “Top flights”, “Sorted by top flights”, “1 stop”, “Nonstop”, “18 hr 20 min”, “13 hr 30 min”, “13 hr 45 min”, “$620”, “$756”, “$757”, “Cheapest from $550”.
selected_candidate:
Air New Zealand, 11:45 AM – 12:05 PM, 18 hr 20 min, 1 stop, SYD–SFO, $620.
constraint_evidence:
- One-way flight: supported (“One way” visible).
- Origin Sydney: supported (“Sydney” visible).
- Destination San Francisco: supported (“San Francisco” visible).
- Departure date March 10, 2026: supported by URL/context and visible as “Tue, Mar 10”.
- Shortest layover: not_visible; no layover-duration sorting/filter/result detail shown.
- Flight search results page: supported.
- Must be a flight option from SYD to SFO: supported by visible route labels.
- Prefer/require shortest layover among options: not_visible in current results; only stop count and total duration shown.
- One-way vs round-trip: supported as one-way.
action_target:
Send exit message to user: “[EXIT]”.
success_signals:
User-facing completion/exit after inspecting the screenshot; no further browser action needed.
failure_signals:
If the intent was to continue searching, exiting now would stop before verifying shortest layover.
uncertainty:
The screenshot does not show layover durations for connecting f</TRAJECTA_DATA_91c1abd0>

task_context.task: navigate: Search for one-way flights from Sydney to San Francisco, departing on March 10, 2026, with the shortest layover.
task_context.title: <TRAJECTA_DATA_91c1abd0>Find Cheap Flights Worldwide & Book Your Ticket - Google Flights</TRAJECTA_DATA_91c1abd0>

observation.title: <TRAJECTA_DATA_91c1abd0>Find Cheap Flights Worldwide & Book Your Ticket - Google Flights</TRAJECTA_DATA_91c1abd0>

args.query: Google Flights results page with shortest layover not visible; selected top flight without layover verification; exit after search results

items.task: navigate: Search for the lowest-priced round-trip flight from Los Angeles to Seoul, departing on April 5, 2026, and returning on April 15, 2026.
items.expected_behavior: The agent should have sorted the flight results by price and selected the lowest-priced round-trip flight for the specified dates.
items.actual_behavior: The agent performed a search for flights from Los Angeles to Seoul for the specified dates. While the search results page displayed various options, the agent did not select the lowest-priced flight, instead exiting while the results were sorted by "top flights." The task specifically required finding the lowest-priced flight.

evidence.claim: The page is currently sorted by 'top flights,' not 'price.'
The agent exited without selecting the lowest-priced flight, failing to fulfill the core requirement of the task.

items.regression_rule: When a task requires finding the 'lowest-priced' item, the agent must ensure the results are sorted by price and that the lowest-priced option is identified and selected. Failure to select the correct item based on the specified criteria constitutes a wrong result.

items.task: find: answer to 'What's the flight option with the latest departure time on the return journey?'
items.expected_behavior: The agent should have navigated to or identified the return flight options and selected the one with the latest departure time from that list.
items.actual_behavior: The agent identified the latest departure time from the "departing flights" (outbound) list instead of the "return journey" list. The agent also provided an incomplete answer.

evidence.claim: The agent failed to recognize that the current view is for "departing flights" (outbound), not the "return journey" requested by the user. The agent also cut off the message mid-sentence.
The screenshot shows "Top departing flights" for the outbound leg, not the return journey.

items.regression_rule: When searching for flight information, the agent must explicitly verify whether it is looking at the outbound or return leg of the journey as requested by the user.

items.task: navigate: Search for a 5-star hotel in New York available for a 3-night stay from January 15, 2026, with a rating of 8.0 or higher.
items.expected_behavior: The agent should have correctly set the destination to New York, the dates to January 15-18, 2026, and applied the necessary filters for a 5-star rating and a guest rating of 8.0 or higher before searching and selecting a suitable hotel.
items.actual_behavior: The agent initiated a search for a hotel in New York, but it failed to set the required dates (January 15, 2026, for 3 nights) and did not apply any filters for star rating or guest rating. The agent then prematurely exited the task by sending an '[EXIT]' message while the date picker was still open and showing the wrong months.

evidence.claim: The agent failed to set the required dates (January 15, 2026, for 3 nights) in the search form. The calendar was left open on the wrong months (February/March 2026).
The agent did not apply any filters for the 5-star rating or the 8.0+ guest rating requirements.
The agent sent an '[EXIT]' message without performing the search or selecting a hotel that met the task criteria.

items.regression_rule: The agent must ensure all search parameters (location, dates, and required filters) are correctly configured before executing the search. Prematurely exiting the task without attempting to fulfill the search criteria is a failure.

evidence.claim: The final screenshot shows Google Flights results for one-way Sydney → San Francisco on Tue, Mar 10, and the page confirms the searched route and date requirements were reached.

The results page is labeled 'Sorted by top flights' and displays options such as '1 stop' and 'Nonstop,' but no layover-duration sort/filter or selected result proving the shortest layover was chosen.
The trajectory exits immediately after reaching the results page, so the required shortest-layover verification is not visibly completed.

args.expected_behavior: After searching Sydney → San Francisco for Mar 10, 2026, the agent should have applied or verified the shortest-layover criterion on the results page and selected a flight that satisfied it before exiting.
args.actual_behavior: The agent reached the correct results page but exited while the page was still sorted by top flights, with no visible evidence that layover duration was checked or minimized.
args.regression_rule: For flight-search tasks that explicitly require the shortest layover, reaching a results page is not enough; the agent must visibly verify or enforce layover duration (e.g. via a layover/stop-duration filter, a sort by layover, or an explicit candidate comparison) before terminating.

result.task: navigate: Search for one-way flights from Sydney to San Francisco, departing on March 10, 2026, with the shortest layover.
result.expected_behavior: After searching Sydney → San Francisco for Mar 10, 2026, the agent should have applied or verified the shortest-layover criterion on the results page and selected a flight that satisfied it before exiting.
result.actual_behavior: The agent reached the correct results page but exited while the page was still sorted by top flights, with no visible evidence that layover duration was checked or minimized.

evidence.claim: The final screenshot shows Google Flights results for one-way Sydney → San Francisco on Tue, Mar 10, and the page confirms the searched route and date requirements were reached.

The results page is labeled 'Sorted by top flights' and displays options such as '1 stop' and 'Nonstop,' but no layover-duration sort/filter or selected result proving the shortest layover was chosen.
The trajectory exits immediately after reaching the results page, so the required shortest-layover verification is not visibly completed.

result.regression_rule: For flight-search tasks that explicitly require the shortest layover, reaching a results page is not enough; the agent must visibly verify or enforce layover duration (e.g. via a layover/stop-duration filter, a sort by layover, or an explicit candidate comparison) before terminating.
